   502→
   503→## 14. Data Flow
   504→
   505→### Tool execution via proxy (end-to-end)
   506→
   507→```
   508→Agent sends LLM request -> Proxy
   509→  -> Step 7: Resolve identity toolset (can_use graph + cache)
   510→  -> Step 7.5: Inject Brain tools into request tools[]
   511→  -> Step 8: Forward to Anthropic, read response
   512→  -> Step 8.5: Classify tool_use blocks
   513→  -> Step 9: Execute all classified tools:
   514→       brain-native: graph queries
   515→       integration: credential resolve -> MCP connect -> tools/call -> sanitize
   516→       unknown: error tool_result
   517→  -> Step 9.5: Merge all tool_results into single message
   518→  -> Step 10: Send follow-up to Anthropic with tool_results
   519→  -> Repeat 8-10 until text response or max 10 iterations
   520→  -> Return final response to agent
   521→```
   522→
   523→### Discovery flow
   524→
   525→```
   526→Admin clicks "Discover" -> POST /mcp-servers/:id/discover?dry_run=true
   527→  -> Load mcp_server record
   528→  -> Resolve credentials (if server has provider link)
   529→  -> Connect to MCP server via mcp-client.ts
   530→  -> Call tools/list
   531→  -> Diff against existing mcp_tool records
   532→  -> Return DiscoveryResult (no DB writes)
   533→  -> Admin reviews, selects tools
   534→  -> POST /mcp-servers/:id/sync with selected tools
   535→  -> Apply: create/update/disable mcp_tool records
   536→  -> Update mcp_server.last_discovery, tool_count
   537→```
   538→
   539→## 15. UI Patterns
   540→
   541→- **Tabs**: Use shadcn Tabs component. Tab state via URL search params (`?tab=tools`) for deep-linking and OAuth2 callback redirects.
   542→- **Tables**: Follow `PoliciesPage` table pattern -- `<table>` with `text-xs`, hover rows, badge for status/risk
   543→- **Dialogs**: Follow `CreatePolicyDialog` pattern -- controlled dialog with form state, async submit
   544→- **Hooks**: Follow `useLearnings` pattern -- `useState` + `useCallback` + `fetch`, return `{ data, isLoading, error, refresh }`
   545→- **Sidebar**: Add "Tool Registry" link following existing `Link` + `matchRoute` + `navItemClass` pattern
   546→- **Risk badges**: color-coded -- low: green, medium: amber, high: red, critical: destructive
   547→- **Status badges**: active: default, disabled: secondary, revoked: destructive, expired: secondary
   548→- **Tool provenance badge**: "manual" for tools without source_server, server name for discovered tools
   549→
   550→## 16. Security Considerations
   551→
   552→- **No plaintext secrets**: API responses use `has_*` boolean indicators, never return encrypted values
   553→- **Encryption**: All credential storage uses AES-256-GCM via existing `encryption.ts` (ADR-066)
   554→- **OAuth2 state**: CSRF protection via `consumeOAuthState()` with 10-minute TTL
   555→- **Provider deletion guard**: Cannot delete provider with active connected accounts
   556→- **MCP server URL validation**: Only `http://` and `https://` URLs. No `file://`, `javascript://`, etc.
   557→- **SSRF consideration**: Admin-provided MCP server URLs require network-level restrictions in production
   558→- **Credential isolation**: MCP client transport headers never logged or returned in API responses
   559→- **Response sanitization**: Integration tool responses stripped of sensitive headers and credential fields (existing `sanitizeIntegrationResponse`)
   560→- **Transport security**: No downgrade from HTTPS to HTTP when falling back from Streamable HTTP to SSE
   561→
   562→## 17. Testing Strategy
   563→
   564→- **Acceptance tests**: Tool execution end-to-end (mock MCP server via InMemoryTransport), discovery flow, OAuth2 callback, tool CRUD, grant CRUD, provider deletion guard, multi-turn loop with mixed tool calls
   565→- **Unit tests**: Tool classification (existing), governance evaluation (existing), risk_level inference from MCP annotations, sync diff algorithm, URL validation, response sanitization (existing), unified result merging
   566→- **Pattern**: Follow existing `tests/acceptance/tool-registry/` structure. Use `createTestUserWithMcp()` for DPoP-authenticated requests.
   567→- **MCP mock**: Use `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`'s `InMemoryTransport` for acceptance tests with a mock MCP server that exposes known tools
   568→
   569→## 18. Quality Attribute Strategies
   570→
   571→| Quality Attribute | Strategy |
   572→|-------------------|----------|
   573→| Performance | Tool resolution cached (60s TTL, ADR-065). MCP connections reused within request. Response truncated at 100KB. |
   574→| Security | AES-256-GCM encryption (ADR-066). Credential isolation. SSRF-safe URL validation. Response sanitization. |
   575→| Reliability | Tool errors returned as tool_results (graceful). Max 10 iterations safety limit. Connection timeout 10s. |
   576→| Maintainability | Ports-and-adapters: MCP client as adapter, tool executor as port. Pure classification functions. |
   577→| Testability | MCP client injectable via factory. InMemoryTransport for mock servers. Pure functions for classification, governance, sanitization. |
   578→| Observability | Wide-event spans per proxy request. Tool execution traces. MCP server status tracking. |
   579→
   580→## 19. Architectural Enforcement
   581→
   582→- **Dependency direction**: `proxy/` depends on `tool-registry/` for types and credential resolution. `tool-registry/` does not depend on `proxy/`. Enforce with import-linter or ESLint import rules.
   583→- **No module-level singletons**: MCP connections are request-scoped. Tool resolution cache is created per handler factory instance, not at module level.
   584→- **Encrypted field naming**: All `_encrypted` fields (ADR-068) enforced by SCHEMAFULL schema -- cannot persist un-suffixed credential field names.
   585→
   586→## 20. External Integrations
   587→
   588→**Contract tests recommended for:**
   589→- **Upstream MCP Servers** (MCP protocol): consumer-driven contracts via Pact-JS to verify tools/list and tools/call response shapes
   590→- **Anthropic Messages API** (HTTP REST): existing proxy tests cover this
   591→- **OAuth2 Providers** (token exchange): consumer-driven contracts via Pact-JS for token endpoint response shape
   592→
   593→Annotation for platform-architect handoff: "Contract tests recommended for upstream MCP server protocol compliance and OAuth2 token exchange endpoints -- consumer-driven contracts (e.g., Pact-JS) to detect breaking changes before production."
   594→

1→# MCP Server Discovery via tools/list -- Architecture
     2→
     3→**Depends on**: architecture.md (Tool Registry page, tool CRUD endpoints, proxy pipeline)
     4→**ADRs**: ADR-070 (discovery architecture), ADR-071 (MCP protocol execution)
     5→
     6→## 1. Overview
     7→
     8→Workspace admins connect external MCP servers by URL. Brain acts as an MCP **client**, calls `tools/list` to discover available tools, and creates `mcp_tool` records from the response. Re-syncs are on-demand -- the admin clicks "Sync" when they want to check for changes. The same MCP client module is used for tool execution in the proxy pipeline (ADR-071).
     9→
    10→## 2. Scope
    11→
    12→### In scope
    13→- **`mcp_server` table**: new SurrealDB entity tracking connected MCP server endpoints
    14→- **MCP client module**: connects to remote MCP servers via SSE or Streamable HTTP transport
    15→- **Discovery service**: calls `tools/list`, maps MCP tool definitions to `mcp_tool` records with risk_level inference
    16→- **Sync service**: diffs discovered tools against existing records -- creates new, updates changed schemas, disables removed
    17→- **Discovery UI**: server connection dialog, tool review screen with selective import, sync status
    18→- **Backend endpoints**: server CRUD, trigger discovery on demand
    19→- **Credential injection**: resolve credentials from linked credential_provider and inject into MCP transport headers
    20→
    21→### Out of scope
    22→- Bidirectional MCP -- Brain does not expose its own tools to the connected server during discovery
    23→- Persistent connections or `listChanged` subscriptions (ADR-070)
    24→- mTLS or custom TLS for MCP connections (standard HTTPS only)
    25→
    26→## 3. Schema: `mcp_server` Table
    27→
    28→Migration: `schema/migrations/0066_mcp_server_table.surql`
    29→
    30→```sql
    31→BEGIN TRANSACTION;
    32→
    33→DEFINE TABLE mcp_server SCHEMAFULL;
    34→DEFINE FIELD name             ON mcp_server TYPE string;
    35→DEFINE FIELD url              ON mcp_server TYPE string;
    36→DEFINE FIELD transport        ON mcp_server TYPE string
    37→  ASSERT $value IN ["sse", "streamable-http"];
    38→DEFINE FIELD workspace        ON mcp_server TYPE record<workspace>;
    39→DEFINE FIELD provider         ON mcp_server TYPE option<record<credential_provider>>;
    40→DEFINE FIELD last_status      ON mcp_server TYPE string
    41→  ASSERT $value IN ["ok", "error"];
    42→DEFINE FIELD server_info      ON mcp_server TYPE option<object> FLEXIBLE;
    43→DEFINE FIELD capabilities     ON mcp_server TYPE option<object> FLEXIBLE;
    44→DEFINE FIELD last_discovery   ON mcp_server TYPE option<datetime>;
    45→DEFINE FIELD last_error       ON mcp_server TYPE option<string>;
    46→DEFINE FIELD tool_count       ON mcp_server TYPE int DEFAULT 0;
    47→DEFINE FIELD created_at       ON mcp_server TYPE datetime DEFAULT time::now();
    48→
    49→DEFINE INDEX mcp_server_workspace      ON mcp_server FIELDS workspace;
    50→DEFINE INDEX mcp_server_workspace_name ON mcp_server FIELDS workspace, name UNIQUE;
    51→
    52→-- Link tools to their source server
    53→DEFINE FIELD OVERWRITE source_server ON mcp_tool TYPE option<record<mcp_server>>;
    54→
    55→COMMIT TRANSACTION;
    56→```
    57→
    58→### Key design choices
    59→
    60→- **`transport` field**: MCP protocol supports two HTTP transports -- legacy SSE (`/sse` endpoint) and the newer Streamable HTTP. Stored so reconnection uses the correct transport.
    61→- **`server_info` + `capabilities`**: Cached from MCP `initialize` handshake. `FLEXIBLE` because the MCP spec allows arbitrary fields. Stored for informational display.
    62→- **`source_server` on `mcp_tool`**: Links discovered tools back to their origin. Manually-created tools have `source_server: NONE`. Enables "re-sync this server" and "which server did this tool come from?" queries.
    63→- **`provider` on `mcp_server`**: Optional link to a credential_provider for authenticated MCP servers. The MCP client injects credentials from this provider's connected_account.
    64→- **Unique constraint**: `workspace + name` prevents duplicate server names within a workspace.
    65→
    66→## 4. MCP Client Module
    67→
    68→File: `app/src/server/tool-registry/mcp-client.ts`
    69→
    70→Uses `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/client` (MIT license, ^1.27.1, already a project dependency):
    71→
    72→### 4.1 Types
    73→
    74→```typescript
    75→import { Client } from "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/client/index.js";
    76→import { SSEClientTransport } from "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/client/sse.js";
    77→import { StreamableHTTPClientTransport } from "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/client/streamableHttp.js";
    78→
    79→type McpConnectionResult = {
    80→  client: Client;
    81→  serverInfo: { name: string; version: string };
    82→  capabilities: Record<string, unknown>;
    83→};
    84→
    85→type McpClientFactory = (
    86→  url: string,
    87→  transport: "sse" | "streamable-http",
    88→  headers?: Record<string, string>,
    89→) => Promise<McpConnectionResult>;
    90→```
    91→
    92→### 4.2 Transport selection strategy
    93→
    94→1. **User selects transport** in the connection dialog (default: `streamable-http`)
    95→2. **Auto-detect fallback**: if `streamable-http` fails with 4xx, retry with `sse` and update stored transport field
    96→3. No stdio transport -- Brain server connects over HTTP only
    97→
    98→### 4.3 Credential injection
    99→
   100→If `mcp_server.provider` is set:
   101→1. Resolve the admin's `connected_account` for the provider (identity from request context)
   102→2. Decrypt credentials via `decryptSecret()`
   103→3. Build auth headers via `buildAuthHeaders()` (reusing `credential-resolver.ts`)
   104→4. Inject as `requestInit.headers` on the MCP transport constructor:
   105→   - `SSEClientTransport({ url, requestInit: { headers } })`
   106→   - `StreamableHTTPClientTransport({ url, requestInit: { headers } })`
   107→
   108→For tool execution in the proxy, credential resolution uses the **proxy identity** (the agent's user), not the admin. The proxy's existing `resolveCredentialsForTool` handles this.
   109→
   110→### 4.4 Connection timeout
   111→
   112→Transport connection timeout: 10 seconds. If the MCP server does not respond within 10 seconds, the connection attempt fails with a descriptive error. Implemented via `AbortController` with `setTimeout`.
   113→
   114→### 4.5 Dependency injection
   115→
   116→The `McpClientFactory` is added to `ServerDependencies` for injection:
   117→
   118→```typescript
   119→// In runtime/types.ts
   120→type ServerDependencies = {
   121→  // ... existing
   122→  mcpClientFactory: McpClientFactory;
   123→};
   124→```
   125→
   126→Production creates real transports. Tests inject a factory backed by `InMemoryTransport` from the MCP SDK.
   127→
   128→## 5. Discovery Service
   129→
   130→File: `app/src/server/tool-registry/discovery.ts`
   131→
   132→### 5.1 Core types
   133→
   134→```typescript
   135→type DiscoveryResult = {
   136→  created: number;
   137→  updated: number;
   138→  disabled: number;
   139→  unchanged: number;
   140→  tools: ToolSyncDetail[];
   141→};
   142→
   143→type ToolSyncDetail = {
   144→  name: string;
   145→  description: string;
   146→  action: "create" | "update" | "disable" | "unchanged";
   147→  risk_level: "low" | "medium" | "high" | "critical";
   148→  changes?: string[];
   149→  input_schema: Record<string, unknown>;
   150→};
   151→```
   152→
   153→### 5.2 Risk level inference
   154→
   155→MCP spec defines tool annotations (hints). Map them to `risk_level`:
   156→
   157→| MCP Annotation | `risk_level` |
   158→|----------------|-------------|
   159→| `readOnlyHint: true` | `low` |
   160→| `idempotentHint: true` (no destructive) | `medium` |
   161→| `destructiveHint: true` | `high` |
   162→| `destructiveHint: true` + no confirmation | `critical` |
   163→| No annotations | `medium` (default) |
   164→
   165→Admin can override inferred risk_level before import.
   166→
   167→### 5.3 Sync algorithm
   168→
   169→```
   170→1. Fetch tools from MCP server via client.listTools()
   171→2. Load existing mcp_tool records WHERE source_server = $server
   172→3. Build lookup maps by tool name
   173→4. For each remote tool:
   174→   a. Not in local -> action: "create"
   175→   b. In local, schema differs -> action: "update" (track changed fields)
   176→   c. In local, identical -> action: "unchanged"
   177→5. For each local tool NOT in remote -> action: "disable"
   178→6. If not dry_run: apply all create/update/disable operations
   179→7. Update mcp_server: last_discovery, tool_count, last_status, server_info, capabilities
   180→```
   181→
   182→### 5.4 Schema diff
   183→
   184→Compare `input_schema` and `description` using deep equality (canonical `JSON.stringify` comparison). Track changed fields in `ToolSyncDetail.changes` (e.g., `["input_schema modified", "description changed"]`).
   185→
   186→### 5.5 Selective import
   187→
   188→The sync endpoint accepts an optional `selected_tools` array. When present, only tools in this list are created/updated. Tools not in the list are skipped (not disabled -- they simply aren't imported). This enables the UI's checkbox-based selective import.
   189→
   190→## 6. Backend Endpoints
   191→
   192→### Route file: `app/src/server/tool-registry/server-routes.ts`
   193→
   194→| Method | Path | Purpose |
   195→|--------|------|---------|
   196→| POST | `/api/workspaces/:wsId/mcp-servers` | Register MCP server (name, url, transport, provider?) |
   197→| GET | `/api/workspaces/:wsId/mcp-servers` | List registered MCP servers with status |
   198→| GET | `/api/workspaces/:wsId/mcp-servers/:serverId` | Server detail (info, capabilities, tool_count) |
   199→| DELETE | `/api/workspaces/:wsId/mcp-servers/:serverId` | Remove server + disable discovered tools |
   200→| POST | `/api/workspaces/:wsId/mcp-servers/:serverId/discover` | Trigger discovery (dry_run query param for preview) |
   201→| POST | `/api/workspaces/:wsId/mcp-servers/:serverId/sync` | Apply sync with optional selected_tools filter |
   202→
   203→### Discovery endpoint flow
   204→
   205→```
   206→POST /mcp-servers/:serverId/discover?dry_run=true
   207→
   208→1. Load mcp_server record
   209→2. Resolve credentials if server has provider link
   210→3. Connect via mcpClientFactory(url, transport, headers?)
   211→4. Call client.listTools()
   212→5. discoverTools(surreal, server, workspace, client, { dryRun: true })
   213→6. Close client connection
   214→7. Return DiscoveryResult with per-tool breakdown
   215→
   216→POST /mcp-servers/:serverId/sync
   217→
   218→1. Same connect + discover flow with dryRun: false
   219→2. Apply selected_tools filter if provided in request body
   220→3. Write changes to DB
   221→4. Close client connection
   222→5. Return DiscoveryResult
   223→```
   224→
   225→### Query functions: `app/src/server/tool-registry/server-queries.ts`
   226→
   227→```typescript
   228→createMcpServer(surreal, workspaceRecord, content)
   229→listMcpServers(surreal, workspaceRecord)
   230→getMcpServerById(surreal, serverRecord)
   231→updateMcpServerStatus(surreal, serverRecord, status, lastError?)
   232→updateMcpServerAfterDiscovery(surreal, serverRecord, toolCount, capabilities, serverInfo)
   233→deleteMcpServer(surreal, serverRecord)
   234→disableToolsBySourceServer(surreal, serverRecord)
   235→listToolsBySourceServer(surreal, serverRecord)
   236→mcpServerNameExists(surreal, workspaceRecord, name)
   237→```
   238→
   239→## 7. UI Components
   240→
   241→### New components (under `app/src/client/components/tool-registry/`)
   242→
   243→```
   244→McpServerSection.tsx          # Server list + "Add Server" button (collapsible section in Tools tab)
   245→AddMcpServerDialog.tsx        # Form: name, url, transport selector, optional provider dropdown
   246→DiscoveryReviewPanel.tsx      # Shows dry_run results: tool list with action badges
   247→DiscoveryReviewToolRow.tsx    # Per-tool row: name, risk badge, action, schema diff toggle
   248→SyncStatusIndicator.tsx       # Connection status: ok (green dot), error (red dot)
   249→```
   250→
   251→### New hook: `app/src/client/hooks/use-mcp-servers.ts`
   252→
   253→```typescript
   254→type UseMcpServersReturn = {
   255→  servers: McpServerListItem[];
   256→  isLoading: boolean;
   257→  error?: string;
   258→  refresh: () => void;
   259→  addServer: (input: AddMcpServerInput) => Promise<McpServerListItem>;
   260→  removeServer: (serverId: string) => Promise<void>;
   261→  discoverTools: (serverId: string) => Promise<DiscoveryResult>;
   262→  syncTools: (serverId: string, selectedTools?: string[]) => Promise<DiscoveryResult>;
   263→};
   264→```
   265→
   266→### Discovery UX flow
   267→
   268→1. Admin clicks **"Add MCP Server"** -> `AddMcpServerDialog` opens
   269→2. Enters name, URL, selects transport (default: streamable-http), optionally links a credential provider
   270→3. On submit -> `POST /mcp-servers` creates record, then immediately `POST /mcp-servers/:id/discover?dry_run=true`
   271→4. **Review panel** appears showing discovered tools with:
   272→   - Tool name + description
   273→   - Inferred `risk_level` badge (from MCP annotations)
   274→   - Action: "new" for first discovery
   275→   - Checkbox per tool (all selected by default) for selective import
   276→5. Admin reviews, optionally unchecks tools, adjusts risk_levels
   277→6. Clicks **"Import Selected"** -> `POST /mcp-servers/:id/sync` with selected tool names
   278→7. Tools appear in the Tools tab, linked to the server via `source_server`
   279→
   280→### Re-sync flow
   281→
   282→1. Admin clicks **"Sync"** on an existing server row
   283→2. `POST /mcp-servers/:id/discover?dry_run=true` -> review panel shows diff:
   284→   - **New**: tools on server not in Brain (action: create)
   285→   - **Updated**: tools with changed schema (expandable diff view)
   286→   - **Removed**: tools in Brain not on server (action: disable)
   287→   - **Unchanged**: collapsed by default
   288→3. Admin confirms -> `POST /mcp-servers/:id/sync`
   289→
   290→## 8. Integration with Existing Architecture
   291→
   292→### How discovery fits into the Tool Registry page
   293→
   294→The Tools tab gains a collapsible **MCP Servers** section above the tool list. Servers are the source of tools, so they belong in the tools context (not a separate tab -- ADR-070).
   295→
   296→```
   297→Tools tab
   298→+-- MCP Servers section (collapsible)
   299→|   +-- Server list (name, url, status indicator, tool_count, last_discovery)
   300→|   +-- "Add MCP Server" button
   301→|   +-- Per-server: "Sync" / "Remove" actions
   302→+-- Separator
   303→+-- Tool list (all tools, both manual and discovered)
   304→    +-- Each tool shows source badge: "manual" or server name
   305→```
   306→
   307→### Tool provenance
   308→
   309→- `source_server: NONE` -> manually registered tool (existing flow)
   310→- `source_server: mcp_server:xxx` -> discovered from MCP server
   311→- UI shows a small badge indicating provenance
   312→- Discovered tools can be edited (risk_level override, status toggle) but schema fields are read-only (managed by sync)
   313→
   314→### Credential chain for discovered tools
   315→
   316→Discovery populates `mcp_tool` records. The tool executor needs credentials to execute them. The credential chain:
   317→
   318→1. **Server-level credentials** (for connecting to the MCP server): resolved from `mcp_server.provider` -> admin's `connected_account`
   319→2. **Tool-level credentials** (for the tool itself, if the MCP server acts as a gateway): resolved from `mcp_tool.provider` -> proxy user's `connected_account`
   320→
   321→For most MCP servers, server-level credentials are sufficient (the MCP server authenticates the client, then proxies API calls). Tool-level credentials are for advanced scenarios where the MCP server requires per-tool auth.
   322→
   323→## 9. Security Considerations
   324→
   325→- **URL validation**: Only allow `http://` and `https://` URLs. No `file://`, `javascript://`, etc.
   326→- **SSRF protection**: Brain server makes outbound HTTP connections to admin-provided URLs. Consider allowlist or network-level restriction for production.
   327→- **Credential isolation**: MCP client headers are injected per-connection, never logged or returned in API responses.
   328→- **Transport downgrade**: Auto-detect fallback from streamable-http to SSE is safe (both are HTTP). No downgrade to unencrypted if original URL is HTTPS.
   329→- **Connection lifecycle**: On-demand only -- no persistent connections. Each discover/sync/execute is short-lived.
   330→
   331→## 10. Testing Strategy
   332→
   333→- **Unit tests**: risk_level inference from MCP annotations, sync diff algorithm, URL validation, transport selection
   334→- **Acceptance tests**: full discovery flow with mock MCP server (InMemoryTransport), sync with schema changes, server CRUD, selective import, credential injection
   335→- **Edge cases**: server unreachable, tools/list returns empty, transport auto-detect fallback, server removal disables tools
   336→

1→# Wave Decisions -- Tool Registry UI (DESIGN)
     2→
     3→## Decision D-01: MCP Protocol Execution Replaces Direct HTTP for Integration Tools
     4→
     5→**Decision**: Replace the current `executeIntegrationTools` HTTP-fetch pattern with MCP protocol execution via `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/client`.
     6→
     7→**Rationale**: The current integration executor expects an `endpoint_url` in the tool input and makes a raw HTTP call. This was a placeholder -- MCP tools don't carry their own endpoint URL. Instead, tools are hosted by MCP servers and executed via the MCP `tools/call` JSON-RPC method. The executor must resolve the tool's `source_server`, connect via MCP client, and call `tools/call`.
     8→
     9→**Alternatives considered**:
    10→- Keep HTTP-fetch pattern and require each tool definition to include an endpoint URL: rejected because MCP tools don't carry endpoint URLs, and it forces admins to duplicate server URLs per-tool.
    11→- Embed a lightweight JSON-RPC client instead of using MCP SDK: rejected because the SDK handles transport negotiation, session management, and protocol framing. Reimplementing this adds maintenance burden with no benefit.
    12→
    13→**Impact**: `tool-executor.ts` `executeIntegrationTools` is replaced. Credential injection moves from HTTP headers to MCP transport headers. The MCP client module is the new adapter boundary.
    14→
    15→## Decision D-02: Connect-Per-Request with Intra-Request Session Reuse
    16→
    17→**Decision**: MCP client connections are established per proxy request, not per tool call. Within a single proxy request, if multiple tool calls target the same MCP server, the connection is reused. Connections are closed when the proxy request completes.
    18→
    19→**Rationale**: Pure connect-per-call (LiteLLM pattern) pays connection + MCP handshake cost for every tool call. With multi-turn loops, a single proxy request may execute 3-5 tool calls against the same server. Reusing the connection within a request amortizes that cost. Connections are NOT held across separate proxy requests -- no persistent connection pool, no module-level singleton state.
    20→
    21→**Alternatives considered**:
    22→- Connect-per-call (LiteLLM pattern): simpler but 100-300ms overhead per call. Acceptable for single calls but compounds in multi-turn loops.
    23→- Persistent connection pool with TTL: lower latency but adds module-level mutable state (violates AGENTS.md), reconnection logic, and cleanup complexity. Over-engineering for current scale.
    24→
    25→**Aligns with**: ADR-070 on-demand connections, AGENTS.md no module-level mutable singletons, Decision 9 from DISCUSS wave.
    26→
    27→## Decision D-03: Unified Tool Execution in Multi-Turn Loop
    28→
    29→**Decision**: The multi-turn loop handles brain-native, integration, and mixed tool calls in a single iteration. Currently the loop has two separate branches (brain-native vs integration). The revised loop processes ALL classified tool calls per iteration: brain-native tools execute via graph queries, integration tools execute via MCP client, unknown tools pass through. All results are combined into a single `tool_result` message for the follow-up LLM request.
    30→
    31→**Rationale**: The current two-branch design cannot handle responses where the LLM calls both a brain-native tool and an integration tool simultaneously. The Anthropic API allows multiple `tool_use` blocks per response. A unified approach handles all combinations.
    32→
    33→**Alternatives considered**:
    34→- Execute brain-native first, integration second, in sequence: rejected because it splits a single response's tool calls across two iterations, doubling LLM round-trips.
    35→- Fail when mixed tool calls detected: rejected because it artificially limits the LLM's ability to call tools from different sources in one turn.
    36→
    37→**Impact**: Proxy loop refactored from two branches to one unified branch with parallel execution.
    38→
    39→## Decision D-04: Tool Resolution Query Extended with source_server
    40→
    41→**Decision**: The `can_use` graph query in `tool-resolver.ts` is extended to also return `source_server` from the `mcp_tool` record. The `ResolvedTool` type gains a `source_server_id` field (raw string ID, not full record). The tool executor uses this to look up the MCP server for execution.
    42→
    43→**Rationale**: The executor needs to know which MCP server hosts each integration tool. Adding this to the existing resolution query avoids an extra DB round-trip per tool call. The `source_server` field is optional (manual tools have NONE).
    44→
    45→**Alternatives considered**:
    46→- Separate query at execution time to look up source_server: rejected because the resolver already traverses the graph for each tool. Adding one field to the SELECT is cheaper than a second query.
    47→- Store server URL directly on mcp_tool: rejected because it duplicates data (URL already on mcp_server) and requires updating every tool when a server URL changes.
    48→
    49→## Decision D-05: MCP Client Module as Injected Dependency
    50→
    51→**Decision**: The MCP client module exposes factory functions, not a singleton. The proxy route creates MCP client connections via a factory injected through `ServerDependencies`. The factory takes `(url, transport, headers)` and returns a connected client. Tests can inject a mock factory.
    52→
    53→**Rationale**: AGENTS.md forbids module-level mutable singletons. The proxy already receives `ServerDependencies` -- the MCP client factory fits this pattern. Testability: acceptance tests inject a mock MCP server via `InMemoryTransport` from the MCP SDK.
    54→
    55→## Decision D-06: Max Tool Use Iterations Increased to 10
    56→
    57→**Decision**: Increase `MAX_TOOL_USE_ITERATIONS` from 5 to 10 in the proxy loop.
    58→
    59→**Rationale**: DISCUSS wave Decision 9 and requirements FR-UI-11 specify a 10-iteration safety limit. Integration tool calls often trigger multi-step workflows (e.g., LLM calls search, then creates, then verifies). 5 iterations is too restrictive for realistic multi-tool workflows. 10 matches the documented requirement.
    60→
    61→## Decision D-07: MCP Server Status Lifecycle
    62→
    63→**Decision**: `mcp_server.last_status` uses two values: `ok` and `error`. Status is updated after every discovery or tool execution attempt. `last_error` stores the most recent error message. There is no intermediate "connecting" status -- connections are short-lived and synchronous from the API caller's perspective.
    64→
    65→**Rationale**: Simple two-state model matches the on-demand connection pattern. A "connecting" state would require background tasks and state cleanup. The admin sees either "last operation succeeded" or "last operation failed with reason X."
    66→

1→# ADR-071: MCP Protocol Tool Execution via Connect-Per-Request
     2→
     3→## Status
     4→Proposed
     5→
     6→## Context
     7→
     8→The proxy pipeline classifies LLM tool calls as brain-native, integration, or unknown (ADR-064, tool-router.ts). Brain-native tools execute via graph queries. Integration tools currently use direct HTTP fetch with `endpoint_url` extracted from the tool's input arguments. This was a placeholder implementation.
     9→
    10→MCP tools do not carry endpoint URLs -- they are hosted by MCP servers and executed via the MCP protocol's `tools/call` JSON-RPC method. The `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` (MIT, ^1.27.1) is already a project dependency (used for the CLI MCP server). Its client module supports SSE and Streamable HTTP transports.
    11→
    12→Three connection lifecycle options exist:
    13→1. **Connect-per-call**: new connection + MCP handshake per tool call (LiteLLM pattern)
    14→2. **Connect-per-request**: connection per proxy request, reused for multiple calls to the same server within that request
    15→3. **Persistent pool**: long-lived connections with TTL, shared across requests
    16→
    17→## Decision
    18→
    19→### MCP protocol execution replaces HTTP fetch
    20→
    21→Integration tool execution uses the MCP SDK client (`@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/client`) instead of raw HTTP fetch. The executor:
    22→1. Resolves `source_server` from the `mcp_tool` record (new field, added via migration)
    23→2. Loads the `mcp_server` record to get URL, transport, and optional credential provider
    24→3. Resolves and decrypts credentials (reusing existing `credential-resolver.ts`)
    25→4. Creates an MCP client transport with credentials injected as HTTP headers
    26→5. Calls `tools/call` with the tool name and arguments
    27→6. Converts the MCP `CallToolResult` to an Anthropic `tool_result` content block
    28→
    29→### Connect-per-request lifecycle
    30→
    31→Connections are scoped to the proxy request lifetime:
    32→- First tool call to a server within a request creates the connection (lazy connect)
    33→- Subsequent calls to the same server within the same request reuse the connection
    34→- All connections are closed when the proxy request completes (in a `finally` block)
    35→- No connections held across requests -- no persistent pool, no module-level state
    36→
    37→A request-scoped `Map<string, Client>` (keyed by mcp_server ID) tracks active connections. This map is a local variable in the proxy handler, not module-level state.
    38→
    39→### MCP client factory as injected dependency
    40→
    41→The MCP client is accessed via a factory function in `ServerDependencies`:
    42→```
    43→createMcpClient: (url, transport, headers?) => Promise<{ client, serverInfo, capabilities }>
    44→```
    45→Tests inject a mock factory backed by `InMemoryTransport`. Production uses real SSE/Streamable HTTP transports.
    46→
    47→## Alternatives Considered
    48→
    49→### Alternative 1: Connect-per-call (LiteLLM pattern)
    50→- **What**: Create a new MCP client connection for every `tools/call` invocation.
    51→- **Expected impact**: Simplest implementation. No connection tracking needed.
    52→- **Why rejected**: Each connection pays 100-300ms for transport setup + MCP `initialize` handshake. In a multi-turn loop with 3-5 tool calls to the same server, this adds 300-1500ms of overhead. The connect-per-request pattern amortizes this to a single connection cost per server per request.
    53→
    54→### Alternative 2: Persistent connection pool with TTL
    55→- **What**: Maintain a pool of MCP client connections with configurable TTL, shared across proxy requests.
    56→- **Expected impact**: Lowest latency -- warm connections for frequent tool calls.
    57→- **Why rejected**: Requires module-level mutable state (connection pool Map), which violates AGENTS.md ("Do NOT use module-level mutable singletons for caching or shared state"). Also adds reconnection logic, health checks, pool size limits, and cleanup on server shutdown. Over-engineering for current scale. Can be added later if latency profiling shows connect-per-request is insufficient -- it's additive, not a rearchitecture.
    58→
    59→## Consequences
    60→- **Positive**: Integration tools execute via standard MCP protocol instead of ad-hoc HTTP
    61→- **Positive**: Connection reuse within a request reduces multi-turn loop latency
    62→- **Positive**: No module-level state -- connections are request-scoped local variables
    63→- **Positive**: Testable via MCP SDK's `InMemoryTransport` (mock MCP server in acceptance tests)
    64→- **Positive**: Credential injection reuses existing `credential-resolver.ts` and `buildAuthHeaders`
    65→- **Negative**: First tool call per server per request pays full connection cost (100-300ms)
    66→- **Negative**: New `source_server` field on `mcp_tool` requires a migration
    67→- **Negative**: MCP SDK is a runtime dependency for the proxy path (already a dependency for CLI)
    68→

1→# ADR-072: Unified Multi-Turn Tool Loop
     2→
     3→## Status
     4→Proposed
     5→
     6→## Context
     7→
     8→The proxy's tool use interception loop (Step 8.5 in `anthropic-proxy-route.ts`) handles brain-native and integration tool calls in separate branches within each iteration. When the LLM response contains `tool_use` blocks, the loop classifies them and enters either the brain-native branch or the integration branch -- but not both.
     9→
    10→The Anthropic Messages API allows multiple `tool_use` blocks per response. An LLM can call `search_entities` (brain-native) and `github.create_issue` (integration) in the same response. The current two-branch design drops one category in this scenario: if `hasBrainNative` is true, the integration branch is skipped entirely.
    11→
    12→Additionally, the current loop has `MAX_TOOL_USE_ITERATIONS = 5`. DISCUSS wave requirements specify 10 iterations as the safety limit (FR-UI-11).
    13→
    14→## Decision
    15→
    16→### Unified execution per iteration
    17→
    18→Each loop iteration processes ALL classified tool calls from the response:
    19→
    20→1. Extract `tool_use` blocks from LLM response
    21→2. Classify all blocks (brain-native, integration, unknown)
    22→3. If all unknown, break (pass through to runtime)
    23→4. Execute brain-native calls via `executeBrainNativeTools` (existing)
    24→5. Execute integration calls via `executeIntegrationToolsViaMcp` (new, MCP protocol)
    25→6. Combine all results into a single `tool_result` message
    26→7. Send follow-up request to LLM with combined results
    27→8. Repeat until LLM produces non-tool-use response or max iterations reached
    28→
    29→Unknown tool calls within a mixed response receive an `is_error: true` tool_result stating the tool is not registered, allowing the LLM to recover gracefully.
    30→
    31→### Max iterations increased to 10
    32→
    33→`MAX_TOOL_USE_ITERATIONS` increased from 5 to 10 to match the documented requirement.
    34→
    35→## Alternatives Considered
    36→
    37→### Alternative 1: Keep separate branches, execute sequentially
    38→- **What**: Run brain-native branch first, then integration branch, in two sub-steps per iteration.
    39→- **Expected impact**: Handles mixed calls but doubles code paths.
    40→- **Why rejected**: Still requires combining results and building a single follow-up message. The unified approach is simpler: one classification pass, parallel execution, one result merge, one follow-up request. Less code, easier to reason about.
    41→
    42→### Alternative 2: Reject mixed tool calls with error
    43→- **What**: If a response contains both brain-native and integration tool calls, return an error tool_result for all of them.
    44→- **Expected impact**: Simplest implementation.
    45→- **Why rejected**: Artificially limits LLM behavior. The LLM reasonably calls tools from different sources when solving a complex task (e.g., "search Brain's graph for the task, then create a GitHub issue"). Rejecting this forces the LLM into unnecessary sequential behavior.
    46→
    47→## Consequences
    48→- **Positive**: Handles all tool call combinations (brain-native only, integration only, mixed, unknown)
    49→- **Positive**: Single code path instead of two branches -- simpler to maintain
    50→- **Positive**: Unknown tools in mixed responses get graceful error results instead of being silently dropped
    51→- **Positive**: Max iterations aligns with documented requirement (10)
    52→- **Negative**: Slightly more complex result merging logic
    53→- **Negative**: Brain-native and integration tools execute sequentially within an iteration (could be parallelized later if latency is an issue)
    54→

     2→
     3→## US-UI-01: Tool Registry Page Shell and Navigation
     4→
     5→### Problem
     6→Priya Sharma is a DevOps lead who manages integrations for a 6-person team. She currently has to use raw API calls or curl commands to manage credential providers, tool access, and governance. She finds it tedious to context-switch between the Brain web UI and terminal for routine integration management.
     7→
     8→### Who
     9→- Workspace admin | Brain web UI | Needs a dedicated page for integration management
    10→
    11→### Solution
    12→A Tool Registry page accessible from the workspace sidebar with tab navigation (Tools, Providers, Accounts, Access) and appropriate empty states.
    13→
    14→### Domain Examples
    15→#### 1: First visit with empty workspace
    16→Priya Sharma opens Brain for the first time after the tool registry backend was deployed. She clicks "Tool Registry" in the sidebar. The page shows an empty state: "No tools registered yet. Register a credential provider to start connecting integrations." with an "Add Provider" button.
    17→
    18→#### 2: Returning admin with existing tools
    19→Priya returns to the workspace which has 6 tools across GitHub and Slack providers. The Tools tab is active by default showing grouped tool lists. She clicks the Providers tab to check connection statuses.
    20→
    21→#### 3: Member sees member-appropriate view
    22→Carlos Mendez, a senior developer, opens the Tool Registry. He sees the Providers tab showing available providers with Connect buttons and the Accounts tab showing his connected accounts. The Access tab is visible but read-only for non-admins.
    23→
    24→### UAT Scenarios (BDD)
    25→
    26→#### Scenario: Tool Registry appears in sidebar
    27→Given Priya Sharma is authenticated as a workspace admin for "Acme Engineering"
    28→When she views the workspace sidebar
    29→Then "Tool Registry" appears as a navigation item below "Policies"
    30→
    31→#### Scenario: Empty state on first visit
    32→Given "Acme Engineering" has no credential providers or mcp_tools
    33→When Priya navigates to /tools
    34→Then the page title reads "Tool Registry"
    35→And an empty state message says "No tools registered yet"
    36→And an "Add Provider" call-to-action button is visible
    37→
    38→#### Scenario: Tab navigation renders correctly
    39→Given "Acme Engineering" has 4 tools and 2 providers
    40→When Priya navigates to /tools
    41→Then four tabs are visible: Tools, Providers, Accounts, Access
    42→And the Tools tab is active by default
    43→
    44→### Acceptance Criteria
    45→- [ ] Route /tools registered in TanStack Router under authenticated layout
    46→- [ ] Sidebar navigation includes "Tool Registry" entry
    47→- [ ] Four tabs render: Tools, Providers, Accounts, Access
    48→- [ ] Empty state with CTA when no tools/providers exist
    49→- [ ] Page accessible via keyboard navigation
    50→
    51→### Outcome KPIs
    52→- **Who**: Workspace admins
    53→- **Does what**: Navigate to Tool Registry without help documentation
    54→- **By how much**: 95% find it on first attempt
    55→- **Measured by**: Sidebar click-through analytics
    56→- **Baseline**: No UI exists (100% API-only)
    57→
    58→### Technical Notes
    59→- Follow existing route pattern in `router.tsx` (TanStack Router)
    60→- Follow sidebar pattern in `WorkspaceSidebar.tsx`
    61→- Use shadcn Tabs component for tab navigation
    62→- Empty state follows emotional design pattern (inviting, guiding)
    63→
    64→---
    65→
    66→## US-UI-02: Browse Tools
    67→
    68→### Problem
    69→Priya Sharma manages a workspace with 15+ integration tools across GitHub, Slack, and Linear. She finds it difficult to understand which tools exist, their risk levels, and how many agents have access, because this information is only available via raw database queries.
    70→
    71→### Who
    72→- Workspace admin | Brain web UI | Needs overview of all registered tools
    73→
    74→### Solution
    75→A tools list view grouped by toolkit with filtering by status and risk_level, search, and per-tool grant counts.
    76→
    77→### Domain Examples
    78→#### 1: Browse grouped tools
    79→Priya opens the Tools tab. She sees 4 tools under "github" header, 2 under "slack", and 3 under "linear". Each row shows: github.create_issue | medium | active | 3 grants | github.
    80→
    81→#### 2: Filter high-risk tools
    82→Priya selects risk_level "high" from the filter dropdown. Only github.merge_pr and linear.delete_issue appear. She verifies each has governance policies attached.
    83→
    84→#### 3: Search for specific tool
    85→Priya types "create" in the search field. github.create_issue, slack.create_channel, and linear.create_issue appear. She clicks github.create_issue to see its detail.
    86→
    87→### UAT Scenarios (BDD)
    88→
    89→#### Scenario: Tools grouped by toolkit
    90→Given "Acme Engineering" has mcp_tools: github.create_issue, github.list_reviews, slack.post_message, slack.list_channels
    91→When Priya opens the Tools tab
    92→Then tools are grouped under headers "github" (2 tools) and "slack" (2 tools)
    93→And each tool row shows name, risk_level badge, status, grant count, and provider name
    94→
    95→#### Scenario: Filter by risk level
    96→Given 5 active tools with risk_levels: 2 low, 2 medium, 1 high
    97→When Priya selects risk_level filter "high"
    98→Then only 1 tool is displayed
    99→
   100→#### Scenario: Search tools by name
   101→Given 8 tools exist across 3 toolkits
   102→When Priya types "merge" in the search field
   103→Then only github.merge_pr is displayed
   104→
   105→#### Scenario: Empty search results
   106→Given 8 tools exist
   107→When Priya types "nonexistent" in the search field
   108→Then the list shows "No tools match your search"
   109→
   110→### Acceptance Criteria
   111→- [ ] Tools fetched from existing backend API and grouped by toolkit field
   112→- [ ] Each tool row displays: name, description (truncated), risk_level badge, status badge, grant count, provider name
   113→- [ ] Dropdown filters for status (active/disabled) and risk_level (low/medium/high)
   114→- [ ] Client-side text search on tool name and description
   115→- [ ] Empty search state with helpful message
   116→- [ ] Risk level badges use color conventions: low=green, medium=amber, high=red
   117→
   118→### Outcome KPIs
   119→- **Who**: Workspace admins
   120→- **Does what**: Find a specific tool by name or risk level

1→# Walking Skeleton Rationale -- Tool Registry UI
     2→
     3→## Skeleton Definition
     4→
     5→The walking skeleton proves: **Can workspace users manage integrations and see their tools, providers, accounts, and grants through the API endpoints?**
     6→
     7→## Thinnest E2E Slice
     8→
     9→```
    10→Admin registers provider (POST /providers)
    11→  -> Provider appears in list (GET /providers)
    12→Member connects account (POST /accounts/connect/:id)
    13→  -> Account appears in dashboard (GET /accounts)
    14→Admin seeds tools (DB precondition)
    15→  -> Tools browsable with grouping (GET /tools)
    16→Admin grants access (POST /tools/:id/grants)
    17→  -> Grant visible in tool detail (GET /tools/:id)
    18→```
    19→
    20→## Why These Four Skeletons
    21→
    22→1. **Provider registration + listing**: Proves the Providers tab has data. Entry point for all subsequent integration management.
    23→2. **Account connection + listing**: Proves the Accounts tab works. Validates credential encryption pipeline end-to-end.
    24→3. **Tool browsing with grouping**: Proves the Tools tab renders meaningful data. Validates the NEW GET /tools endpoint with counts.
    25→4. **Grant creation + detail**: Proves the Access tab works. Validates the NEW grant management endpoints.
    26→
    27→## Litmus Test
    28→
    29→| Question | Answer |
    30→|----------|--------|
    31→| Title describes user goal? | "Admin manages integrations end-to-end" -- yes |
    32→| Given/When describe user actions? | "Admin registers provider", "Member connects account" -- yes |
    33→| Then describe user observations? | "Provider appears in list", "Account shows active status" -- yes |
    34→| Non-technical stakeholder confirms? | "Yes, I can see my providers, connect accounts, browse tools, and manage access" |
    35→
    36→## What Is NOT in the Skeleton
    37→
    38→- OAuth2 browser redirect (cannot test in acceptance suite)
    39→- MCP Server Discovery (deferred per wave decision)
    40→- Governance policy attachment (Release 2 scope)
    41→- UI-specific behavior (keyboard navigation, empty states, badges)
    42→
    43→## Implementation Sequence
    44→
    45→Enable skeletons in order (each builds on previous):
    46→1. Skeleton 1: Provider CRUD (exercises existing endpoint)
    47→2. Skeleton 2: Account connection (exercises existing endpoint with DPoP auth)
    48→3. Skeleton 3: Tool browsing (requires NEW GET /tools endpoint)
    49→4. Skeleton 4: Grant management (requires NEW grant endpoints)
    50→

1→# DISTILL Wave Decisions -- Tool Registry UI
     2→
     3→## Decision 1: HTTP endpoints as driving ports, not DB queries
     4→
     5→**Decision**: All acceptance tests exercise HTTP API endpoints (the driving ports the UI calls), not internal DB queries or service functions.
     6→**Rationale**: The existing `tests/acceptance/tool-registry/` suite tested the data layer via DB seeding. The UI tests must prove the HTTP contract works because that is what the React client consumes. Testing at a different boundary than the UI uses creates Testing Theater.
     7→**Impact**: Walking skeleton tests call `POST /providers`, `GET /tools`, etc. DB queries are only used for Given-step preconditions and Then-step verification of side effects (credential deletion).
     8→
     9→## Decision 2: DPoP auth for account endpoints, session auth for provider/tool endpoints
    10→
    11→**Decision**: Account endpoints (`connect`, `list accounts`, `revoke`) use `mcpFetch` (DPoP-authenticated) because they require `X-Brain-Identity` header. Provider and tool endpoints use session `headers` because they only require workspace scope.
    12→**Rationale**: The existing route handlers extract identity from `X-Brain-Identity` for account operations but only need workspace resolution for provider and tool operations. Tests must match the actual auth mechanism.
    13→**Impact**: Test kit provides both `createTestUserWithMcp()` (for DPoP auth) and session `user.headers` (for workspace-scoped requests).
    14→
    15→## Decision 3: toolEncryptionKey configured via suite options
    16→
    17→**Decision**: The test suite passes `toolEncryptionKey` via `configOverrides` to enable credential encryption in test server.
    18→**Rationale**: Without this key, `POST /accounts/connect` returns 500 "encryption not configured". The key must be at least 32 bytes per AES-256-GCM requirements.
    19→**Impact**: `setupToolRegistrySuite` sets `toolEncryptionKey: "test-encryption-key-32-bytes-long!"` in suite options.
    20→
    21→## Decision 4: UI-only acceptance criteria deferred to component tests
    22→
    23→**Decision**: Acceptance criteria that are purely UI concerns (keyboard navigation, badge colors, client-side filtering, dialog form layout) are not tested at the HTTP acceptance level.
    24→**Rationale**: These criteria (AC-01b, AC-01c, AC-01e, AC-02c-e, AC-02g, AC-03a, AC-03d, AC-04a-d) involve DOM rendering and user interaction that cannot be verified through API responses. They belong in component tests (React Testing Library) or E2E browser tests.
    25→**Impact**: 8 of 45 acceptance criteria are covered by component tests instead of acceptance tests. All data-returning and mutation criteria are covered.
    26→
    27→## Decision 5: OAuth2 tested at initiation only
    28→
    29→**Decision**: OAuth2 acceptance tests verify the redirect URL is generated correctly (status 200 with redirect_url and state). The full redirect-callback-exchange flow is not testable in the acceptance suite.
    30→**Rationale**: Browser redirects to external IdP cannot be simulated in the in-process server test harness. The callback endpoint can be tested separately with a mock IdP in integration tests.
    31→**Impact**: AC-06c/d/e (callback handling) are deferred to integration tests with mock IdP.
    32→
    33→## Decision 6: Separate test directory from existing tool-registry tests
    34→
    35→**Decision**: Tests live in `tests/acceptance/tool-registry-ui/` (new directory), not in the existing `tests/acceptance/tool-registry/`.
    36→**Rationale**: The existing suite tests the backend data layer (tool schema, proxy injection, credential brokerage, governance enforcement). The UI tests exercise HTTP API contracts for UI consumption. Different testing purpose = different test suite.
    37→**Impact**: New test kit in `tool-registry-ui-test-kit.ts` composes shared fixtures but has its own domain helpers for HTTP-level operations.
    38→

121→- **By how much**: Under 10 seconds (vs 30+ seconds via API query)
   122→- **Measured by**: Time from page load to filter/search action
   123→- **Baseline**: No browsing UI exists
   124→
   125→### Technical Notes
   126→- Follow list pattern from learnings-page.tsx (LearningList + LearningFilters)
   127→- Group by toolkit field; collapse/expand groups
   128→- Grant count from subquery: `SELECT count() FROM can_use WHERE out = $parent.id`
   129→- Risk level badge colors: low=green, medium=amber, high=red (shadcn Badge + variant)

   133→## US-UI-03: Register Credential Provider
   134→
   135→### Problem
   136→Priya Sharma needs to register OAuth2 and API key providers so her team's agents can use GitHub and internal APIs. Currently she must construct POST requests manually with correct JSON payloads, remembering which fields are required for each auth method.
   137→
   138→### Who
   139→- Workspace admin | Brain web UI | Needs to register credential providers without API calls
   140→
   141→### Solution
   142→An "Add Provider" dialog with a form that adapts its fields based on the selected auth_method.
   143→
   144→### Domain Examples
   145→#### 1: Register OAuth2 provider (GitHub)
   146→Priya clicks "Add Provider", selects auth_method "oauth2". The form reveals: name, display_name, authorization_url, token_url, client_id, client_secret, scopes. She fills in GitHub's OAuth app credentials and clicks "Create Provider". The provider appears in the list.
   147→
   148→#### 2: Register API key provider (Internal API)
   149→Priya clicks "Add Provider", selects auth_method "api_key". The form shows only: name, display_name. No OAuth fields are visible. She enters "internal-api" and "Internal API", clicks "Create Provider".
   150→
   151→#### 3: Duplicate name rejected
   152→Priya tries to register another provider named "github". The form shows inline error: "A provider named 'github' already exists" and the submit button remains disabled.
   153→
   154→### UAT Scenarios (BDD)
   155→
   156→#### Scenario: Adaptive form for OAuth2
   157→Given Priya clicks "Add Provider"
   158→When she selects auth_method "oauth2"
   159→Then the form displays fields: name, display_name, authorization_url, token_url, client_id, client_secret, scopes
   160→And client_secret field uses password input masking
   161→
   162→#### Scenario: Adaptive form for API key
   163→Given Priya clicks "Add Provider"
   164→When she selects auth_method "api_key"
   165→Then the form displays only: name, display_name
   166→And no OAuth-specific fields are visible
   167→
   168→#### Scenario: Successful provider creation
   169→Given Priya fills the OAuth2 form with name "github", display_name "GitHub", valid authorization_url, token_url, client_id "Ov23liABC123", client_secret, and scopes "repo,read:org"
   170→When she clicks "Create Provider"
   171→Then the dialog closes
   172→And "GitHub" appears in the provider list
   173→And a success toast confirms "Provider created"
   174→
   175→#### Scenario: Duplicate name validation
   176→Given credential_provider "github" exists in the workspace
   177→When Priya enters name "github" in the Add Provider form
   178→Then inline error appears: "A provider named 'github' already exists"
   179→And the "Create Provider" button is disabled
   180→
   181→### Acceptance Criteria
   182→- [ ] Dialog form with auth_method selector (oauth2, api_key, bearer, basic)
   183→- [ ] Form fields adapt based on auth_method selection
   184→- [ ] Client_secret field masked as password input
   185→- [ ] Inline validation: required fields, duplicate name check
   186→- [ ] On success: dialog closes, provider list refreshes, success toast
   187→- [ ] On error: form data preserved, error message displayed
   188→
   189→### Outcome KPIs
   190→- **Who**: Workspace admins
   191→- **Does what**: Register a credential provider via UI
   192→- **By how much**: Under 2 minutes (vs 5+ minutes constructing API calls)
   193→- **Measured by**: Time from "Add Provider" click to successful creation
   194→- **Baseline**: API-only (manual JSON construction)
   195→
   196→### Technical Notes
   197→- Follow dialog pattern from CreatePolicyDialog or CreateDialog in learnings
   198→- POST to existing credential provider registration endpoint
   199→- Client secret never returned in API response (backend enforces)
   200→- Validate authorization_url and token_url as valid URLs on blur
   201→

   204→## US-UI-04: Connect Account (Static Credentials)
   205→
   206→### Problem
   207→Carlos Mendez wants agents to use Internal API tools on his behalf. He currently has no way to securely provide his API key through the UI -- he would need to make raw API calls and handle encryption details himself.
   208→
   209→### Who
   210→- Workspace member | Brain web UI | Needs to connect accounts with API key, bearer, or basic auth
   211→
   212→### Solution

   214→
   215→### Domain Examples

   218→
   219→#### 2: Connect with basic auth

   221→
   222→#### 3: Connect with bearer token

   224→
   225→### UAT Scenarios (BDD)
   226→
   227→#### Scenario: API key connection form

   229→When Carlos clicks "Connect" on Internal API
   230→Then a dialog shows an API Key field (password-masked)
   231→And text explains "Your key will be encrypted and stored securely"
   232→
   233→#### Scenario: Basic auth connection form
   234→Given credential_provider "legacy-service" with auth_method "basic" exists
   235→When Carlos clicks "Connect" on Legacy Service
   236→Then a dialog shows username and password fields
   237→
   238→#### Scenario: Successful static connection

   240→When he clicks "Connect"
   241→Then the dialog closes
   242→And Internal API shows status "Connected" in the Providers tab

   250→### Acceptance Criteria
   251→- [ ] Connect dialog adapts to auth_method: api_key (1 field), bearer (1 field), basic (2 fields)
   252→- [ ] Credential fields use password input masking
   253→- [ ] Security messaging: "Your credentials will be encrypted and stored securely"
   254→- [ ] Required field validation prevents empty submission
   255→- [ ] On success: dialog closes, provider status updates, success toast
   256→- [ ] On error: form data preserved, error message displayed
   257→
   258→### Outcome KPIs
   259→- **Who**: Workspace members
   260→- **Does what**: Connect an account to a static credential provider
   261→- **By how much**: Under 30 seconds per provider
   262→- **Measured by**: Time from "Connect" click to "Connected" status
   263→- **Baseline**: No UI path exists

   266→- POST to existing account connection endpoint

   272→## US-UI-05: Grant Tool Access
   273→
   274→### Problem
   275→Priya Sharma needs to control which agents can use which integration tools. Without a UI, she must construct RELATE queries or API calls with exact record IDs to create can_use edges, and has no visual way to see who has access to what.
   276→
   277→### Who
   278→- Workspace admin | Brain web UI | Needs to grant and view tool access per identity
   279→

   337→## US-UI-06: Connect Account (OAuth2 Flow)
   338→
   339→### Problem
   340→Carlos Mendez wants agents to create GitHub issues on his behalf, but GitHub requires OAuth2 authorization. He has no way to initiate the OAuth consent flow from the Brain UI -- the redirect URL handling and token exchange require manual API orchestration.
   341→
   342→### Who
   343→- Workspace member | Brain web UI | Needs to connect accounts via OAuth2 consent flow
   344→
   345→### Solution
   346→A Connect button that shows scope summary, redirects to provider's authorization URL, and handles the callback to store encrypted tokens.
   347→
   348→### Domain Examples
   349→#### 1: Successful GitHub OAuth2 connection
   350→Carlos clicks "Connect" on GitHub. A dialog shows requested scopes (repo, read:org) and explains credentials will be stored securely. He clicks "Continue to GitHub", authorizes at GitHub's consent screen, and is redirected back. GitHub now shows "Connected".
   351→
   352→#### 2: User denies consent
   353→Carlos clicks "Connect" on Slack, is redirected to Slack's OAuth page, but clicks "Deny". He returns to the Providers tab with a toast: "Connection cancelled". No partial state is created.
   354→
   355→#### 3: Token exchange failure
   356→Carlos authorizes at GitHub, but the token exchange fails (e.g., client_secret mismatch). He returns to the Providers tab with an error toast: "Could not complete connection. Please contact your workspace admin." GitHub shows "Not connected".
   357→
   358→### UAT Scenarios (BDD)
   359→
   360→#### Scenario: OAuth2 pre-redirect confirmation
   361→Given credential_provider "github" with auth_method "oauth2" and scopes "repo,read:org"
   362→When Carlos clicks "Connect" on GitHub
   363→Then a confirmation dialog shows scopes: repo, read:org
   364→And text explains "You will be redirected to GitHub to authorize Brain"
   365→
   366→#### Scenario: Successful OAuth2 connection
   367→Given Carlos clicks "Continue to GitHub" in the confirmation dialog
   368→And authorizes Brain at GitHub's consent screen
   369→When GitHub redirects back to Brain's callback URL
   370→Then the Providers tab shows GitHub as "Connected"
   371→And a success toast confirms "GitHub account connected"
   372→
   373→#### Scenario: OAuth2 consent denied
   374→Given Carlos is redirected to GitHub's authorization URL
   375→When he denies consent
   376→Then he returns to the Providers tab
   377→And a toast shows "Connection cancelled"
   378→And GitHub remains "Not connected"
   379→
   380→#### Scenario: OAuth2 token exchange failure
   381→Given Carlos authorizes at GitHub
   382→When the token exchange fails
   383→Then he returns to the Providers tab
   384→And an error toast shows "Could not complete connection. Please contact your workspace admin."
   385→
   386→### Acceptance Criteria
   387→- [ ] Pre-redirect dialog shows provider name, requested scopes, and security explanation
   388→- [ ] "Continue to {provider}" button initiates OAuth2 redirect
   389→- [ ] Callback URL handles success: creates connected_account, redirects to Providers tab
   390→- [ ] Callback URL handles error: shows toast, no partial state
   391→- [ ] Consent denial returns user with "Connection cancelled" message
   392→
   393→### Outcome KPIs
   394→- **Who**: Workspace members
   395→- **Does what**: Connect OAuth2 accounts (GitHub, Slack, Linear) via UI
   396→- **By how much**: Under 60 seconds from click to "Connected" status
   397→- **Measured by**: Time from "Connect" click to callback completion
   398→- **Baseline**: No UI path exists
   399→
   400→### Technical Notes
   401→- Backend generates authorization URL with state param (CSRF protection)
   402→- Callback route exchanges code for tokens server-side
   403→- Redirect URI must be configured per provider (admin responsibility)
   404→- Handle popup-blocked browsers: consider same-window redirect vs popup
   405→

   408→## US-UI-07: Connected Accounts Dashboard
   409→
   410→### Problem
   411→Carlos Mendez has connected 4 provider accounts over time. He has no visibility into which are still active, which expired, or how to revoke access. He would need to query the database to find his connected_account records.
   412→
   413→### Who
   414→- Workspace member | Brain web UI | Needs visibility into connected accounts with revoke/reconnect actions
   415→
   416→### Solution
   417→An Accounts tab listing all connected accounts for the current identity with status badges and contextual actions (Revoke for active, Reconnect for expired/revoked).
   418→
   419→### Domain Examples
   420→#### 1: View mixed-status accounts

421→Carlos opens the Accounts tab. He sees: GitHub (active, 2026-03-20), Slack (active, 2026-03-23), Internal API (active, 2026-03-22), Legacy Service (expired, 2026-03-15). Active accounts show "Revoke" buttons, expired shows "Reconnect".
   422→
   423→#### 2: Revoke active account
   424→Carlos clicks "Revoke" on GitHub. A confirmation dialog warns: "This will permanently delete your stored credentials. Agents will no longer be able to use GitHub tools on your behalf." He confirms. GitHub status changes to "revoked".
   425→
   426→#### 3: Reconnect expired account
   427→Carlos clicks "Reconnect" on Legacy Service (auth_method: basic). The connection form appears with username and password fields. After entering new credentials, the account returns to "active".
   428→
   429→### UAT Scenarios (BDD)
   430→
   431→#### Scenario: View connected accounts
   432→Given Carlos has connected accounts: GitHub (active), Slack (active), Legacy Service (expired)
   433→When he opens the Accounts tab
   434→Then all 3 accounts are listed
   435→And each shows provider display_name, status badge, and connected_at date
   436→And active accounts show "Revoke" button
   437→And expired account shows "Reconnect" button
   438→
   439→#### Scenario: Revoke connected account
   440→Given Carlos has an active GitHub connected account
   441→When he clicks "Revoke" on GitHub
   442→Then a confirmation dialog warns about permanent credential deletion
   443→When he clicks "Revoke Access"
   444→Then GitHub status changes to "revoked" with immediate UI update
   445→And a success toast confirms "GitHub account disconnected"
   446→
   447→#### Scenario: Reconnect expired account

   549→## US-UI-09: MCP Server Connection
   550→
   551→### Problem
   552→Priya Sharma needs to connect her workspace to external MCP servers (such as a company-hosted GitHub MCP server at `https://mcp.acme.dev/github`) so that Brain can discover and use the tools those servers expose. Currently, every `mcp_tool` record must be created manually with exact JSON schemas -- a tedious, error-prone process that does not scale when a server exposes 20+ tools.
   553→
   554→### Who
   555→- Workspace admin | Brain web UI | Needs to register external MCP server endpoints and trigger tool discovery
   556→
   557→### Solution
   558→An "Add MCP Server" dialog in the Tools tab's server section, where the admin enters a URL, selects transport, optionally links a credential provider for authenticated servers, and saves the connection.
   559→
   560→### Domain Examples
   561→#### 1: Connect unauthenticated MCP server
   562→Priya clicks "Add MCP Server" in the Tools tab. She enters name "GitHub Tools", URL "https://mcp.acme.dev/github", and leaves transport as "Streamable HTTP" (default). No credential provider is selected because the server requires no authentication. She clicks "Connect". The server appears in the MCP Servers section with status "ok" and 0 tools.
   563→
   564→#### 2: Connect authenticated MCP server
   565→Priya clicks "Add MCP Server", enters name "Jira Tools", URL "https://mcp.acme.dev/jira", selects transport "SSE", and links credential provider "jira-api-key" from the dropdown. She clicks "Connect". Brain connects using the decrypted API key from Priya's connected account for that provider.
   566→
   567→#### 3: Connection failure with helpful error
   568→Priya enters URL "https://mcp.acme.dev/nonexistent" and clicks "Connect". After a brief spinner, the dialog shows: "Could not connect to MCP server. The server did not respond at this URL. Verify the URL and try again." The server is saved with status "error" and last_error populated.
   569→
   570→### UAT Scenarios (BDD)
   571→
   572→#### Scenario: Register unauthenticated MCP server
   573→Given Priya is on the Tools tab in "Acme Engineering" workspace
   574→When she clicks "Add MCP Server"
   575→And enters name "GitHub Tools" and URL "https://mcp.acme.dev/github"
   576→And selects transport "Streamable HTTP"
   577→And clicks "Connect"
   578→Then the server is created with status "ok"
   579→And the MCP Servers section shows "GitHub Tools" with 0 tools
   580→
   581→#### Scenario: Register authenticated MCP server
   582→Given credential_provider "jira-api-key" exists with auth_method "api_key"
   583→And Priya has a connected account for "jira-api-key"
   584→When she clicks "Add MCP Server"
   585→And enters name "Jira Tools", URL "https://mcp.acme.dev/jira", transport "SSE"
   586→And selects credential provider "jira-api-key"
   587→And clicks "Connect"
   588→Then the server is created
   589→And Brain connected using the decrypted credential from Priya's account
   590→
   591→#### Scenario: Connection failure shows actionable error
   592→Given Priya enters URL "https://mcp.acme.dev/nonexistent"
   593→When she clicks "Connect"
   594→Then the dialog shows an error: "Could not connect to MCP server"
   595→And suggests verifying the URL
   596→And the server is saved with status "error"
   597→
   598→#### Scenario: Transport auto-detect fallback
   599→Given Priya enters a URL for an MCP server that only supports SSE transport
   600→When she selects "Streamable HTTP" and clicks "Connect"
   601→And the connection fails with a 4xx response
   602→Then Brain automatically retries with SSE transport
   603→And the server is saved with transport "sse" and status "ok"
   604→
   605→#### Scenario: Duplicate server name rejected
   606→Given mcp_server "GitHub Tools" exists in the workspace
   607→When Priya enters name "GitHub Tools" in the Add MCP Server dialog
   608→Then inline error appears: "A server named 'GitHub Tools' already exists"
   609→
   610→### Acceptance Criteria
   611→- [ ] "Add MCP Server" button visible in Tools tab MCP Servers section
   612→- [ ] Dialog with fields: name, URL, transport selector (Streamable HTTP / SSE), optional credential provider dropdown
   613→- [ ] URL validation: only http:// and https:// allowed
   614→- [ ] Duplicate server name validation (unique per workspace)
   615→- [ ] On success: dialog closes, server appears in list with status indicator
   616→- [ ] On connection failure: error message displayed with guidance, server saved with "error" status
   617→- [ ] Transport auto-detect: fallback from Streamable HTTP to SSE on 4xx failure
   618→
   619→### Outcome KPIs
   620→- **Who**: Workspace admins
   621→- **Does what**: Register an external MCP server via UI
   622→- **By how much**: Under 30 seconds per server connection
   623→- **Measured by**: Time from "Add MCP Server" click to server appearing in list
   624→- **Baseline**: No server connection capability exists
   625→
   626→### Technical Notes
   627→- POST to `/api/workspaces/:wsId/mcp-servers` creates record
   628→- After creation, server attempts connection + `initialize` handshake to validate URL
   629→- Transport auto-detect: try streamable-http first, fall back to SSE on 4xx
   630→- Credential injection: if provider linked, decrypt credentials from connected_account and inject as HTTP headers
   631→- `mcp_server` table requires migration (see architecture-mcp-discovery.md)
   632→- Depends on: MCP client module (`mcp-client.ts`) using `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/client`
   633→- ADR-070: on-demand connections only (no persistent subscriptions)

   637→## US-UI-10: Tool Discovery and Import
   638→
   639→### Problem
   640→After connecting an MCP server, Priya Sharma needs to discover what tools it exposes and selectively import them into Brain's tool registry. Without this, she would have to manually inspect the MCP server's `tools/list` response and create each `mcp_tool` record by hand -- copying tool names, descriptions, and JSON schemas one at a time.
   641→
   642→### Who
   643→- Workspace admin | Brain web UI | Needs to preview and selectively import tools from connected MCP servers
   644→
   645→### Solution
   646→A discovery flow triggered per server: Brain calls `tools/list`, shows the admin a review panel with tool names, inferred risk levels, and planned actions (new/update/disable), then applies selected changes on confirmation.
   647→
   648→### Domain Examples
   649→#### 1: First discovery -- all new tools
   650→Priya clicks "Discover" on the "GitHub Tools" server. Brain connects, calls `tools/list`, and returns 12 tools. The review panel shows all 12 as "New" with inferred risk_levels: 5 low (read-only hints), 4 medium (no annotations), 2 high (destructive hints), 1 critical (destructive, no confirmation). All are selected by default. Priya unchecks `github.admin_delete_repo` (critical), then clicks "Import Selected". 11 tools are created as `mcp_tool` records linked to this server.
   651→
   652→#### 2: Re-sync with changes
   653→A month later, the GitHub MCP server has added 2 new tools and updated the schema for `github.create_pr`. Priya clicks "Sync" on "GitHub Tools". The review panel shows: 2 new, 1 updated (expandable diff showing schema changes), 0 disabled, 9 unchanged. She confirms and all 3 changes are applied.
   654→
   655→#### 3: Server removed tools
   656→The GitHub MCP server deprecated `github.legacy_search`. Priya clicks "Sync". The review panel shows 1 tool as "Disabled" -- it will be set to status "disabled" but not deleted. She confirms. The tool remains in Brain's registry with status "disabled" and is no longer injected by the proxy.
   657→
   658→### UAT Scenarios (BDD)
   659→
   660→#### Scenario: First discovery shows all tools as new
   661→Given mcp_server "GitHub Tools" is connected with status "ok"
   662→And the server exposes 12 tools via tools/list
   663→When Priya clicks "Discover" on "GitHub Tools"
   664→Then a review panel shows 12 tools with action "new"
   665→And each tool shows name, description, and inferred risk_level badge
   666→And all tools are selected by default
   667→
   668→#### Scenario: Selective import
   669→Given the discovery review panel shows 12 new tools
   670→And Priya unchecks "github.admin_delete_repo"
   671→When she clicks "Import Selected"
   672→Then 11 mcp_tool records are created with source_server linking to "GitHub Tools"
   673→And "github.admin_delete_repo" is not created
   674→And "GitHub Tools" server shows tool_count = 11
   675→
   676→#### Scenario: Re-sync shows diff
   677→Given "GitHub Tools" has 11 imported tools
   678→And the server now exposes 13 tools (2 new, 1 with updated schema, 10 unchanged)
   679→When Priya clicks "Sync" on "GitHub Tools"
   680→Then the review panel shows: 2 "new", 1 "updated", 0 "disabled", 10 "unchanged"
   681→And the "updated" tool has an expandable diff showing schema changes
   682→
   683→#### Scenario: Risk level inferred from MCP annotations
   684→Given the MCP server tool "github.delete_repo" has annotation destructiveHint: true
   685→And tool "github.list_repos" has annotation readOnlyHint: true
   686→When the discovery review panel displays
   687→Then "github.delete_repo" shows risk_level "high"
   688→And "github.list_repos" shows risk_level "low"
   689→
   690→#### Scenario: Admin overrides inferred risk level
   691→Given the discovery review shows "github.create_pr" with inferred risk_level "medium"
   692→When Priya changes the risk_level to "high" in the review panel
   693→And clicks "Import Selected"
   694→Then the created mcp_tool has risk_level "high"
   695→
   696→### Acceptance Criteria
   697→- [ ] "Discover" button on each server row triggers dry-run discovery
   698→- [ ] Review panel shows per-tool breakdown: name, description, risk_level badge, action (new/update/disable/unchanged)
   699→- [ ] Checkboxes for selective import (all selected by default for "new"; all selected for "update")
   700→- [ ] "Unchanged" tools collapsed by default in review panel
   701→- [ ] Risk level inferred from MCP tool annotations (readOnlyHint, destructiveHint, idempotentHint)
   702→- [ ] Admin can override inferred risk_level before import
   703→- [ ] "Import Selected" applies changes and updates server tool_count
   704→- [ ] Sync shows schema diff for updated tools (expandable)
   705→
   706→### Outcome KPIs
   707→- **Who**: Workspace admins
   708→- **Does what**: Import tools from an MCP server without manual JSON creation
   709→- **By how much**: Under 2 minutes for 20+ tools (vs 30+ minutes manually)
   710→- **Measured by**: Time from "Discover" click to tools appearing in Tools tab
   711→- **Baseline**: Manual tool creation only (no discovery capability)
   712→
   713→### Technical Notes
   714→- POST `/api/workspaces/:wsId/mcp-servers/:serverId/discover?dry_run=true` returns DiscoveryResult
   715→- POST `/api/workspaces/:wsId/mcp-servers/:serverId/sync` applies changes
   716→- Risk inference: readOnlyHint=true -> low, destructiveHint=true -> high, destructiveHint+no confirmationHint -> critical, default -> medium
   717→- Sync algorithm: compare by tool name, deep-equal on input_schema + description for change detection
   718→- Discovered tools get `source_server` field linking back to mcp_server record
   719→- Depends on: US-UI-09 (server must be connected first)
   720→
